refs.note: Verres's praetorian-edict pattern of new institutions "for the cause of one man" (Annian women's-inheritance edict, the temple-of-Castor contract): the same praetor rewriting the Sicilian tithe-system. Book 3 takes the praetorship's fingerprint and lifts it to provincial scale.
Apronius's character (the gulf of vices, dancing naked at banquets) summarised at the actio prima; Book 3 unfolds it.

"Look at the temple of Castor; then, if you dare, make mention of innocence. Look at the erasures in your codex on Sthenius of Thermae's entry." Direct callback to the temple-of-Castor and Sthenius episodes of Books 1-2.
The Liparenses ransoming themselves from pirates under Verres's praetorship --- a back-reference to the pirate themes of Book 1 (the Lampsacum sea-route).

The contrast between equestrian and senatorial juries ("Before, when the equestrian order judged..."): a long arc of late-republican judicial politics. Cicero's argument is a charged reminder that the senatorial recovery of the courts (under the Sullan reforms) is on trial in the Verrines.
Comparison of Verres's tithe-prices with C. Norbanus's: a piece of provincial-financial history, with the tithe-price record itself produced as evidence.
Demographic numbers from the public records of the cities: Leontine, Mutycensian, Herbitan, Agyrine fields' farmer counts.
Archonidas of Helorus's testimony at the actio prima.
Cicero's barb at "Alba" (the gladiator-buffoon broker) marks the contemporary corruption-economy of Roman trials; the figure is local.
The quaestorian and legateship embezzlements summarised at the start of Book 1 are recalled as the precedent for the praetorian thefts; Book 3 calls Cicero's pattern a fourth-act repetition.
Cicero's appeal to his own clerks L. Mamilius and L. Sergius is the ad hominem for the integrity of his own quaestorship at Lilybaeum 75 BC.
L. Piso Frugi's lex Calpurnia repetundis (149 BC) cited as the founding statute of the standing-court system that holds Verres.
